meta.name: Marathon Novice
meta.subtitle: Complete your first 26.2 miles
meta.description: Your first marathon in 20 weeks. Influenced by the Hal Higdon Novice 1 structure — four days of running, with the Sunday long run as the centrepiece of the plan. The goal is to finish, not to set a time. Walk breaks are explicitly permitted and encouraged.
meta.coach_notes: The marathon will test you in ways you cannot anticipate from training. The long run on Sunday is everything in this plan — do not miss it, do not run it too fast, and do not underestimate it. If you need to walk, walk. The finish line counts the same regardless of how you got there.

weeks.title: Week 1
weeks.note: First week of your marathon journey. The only goal this week is to run the four sessions and arrive at Monday feeling healthy. The long run of 13km should feel comfortable — if it does not, this plan needs more base time first.

nut.d: 500ml water. Rest day — recovery is training.

sessions.det: 7km easy · 5:50–6:15/km · Conversational — Easy effort means conversational pace. This is recovery — your body adapts to training during rest, not during hard sessions.

Pull-ups 2x12 · DB row 2x12 · Push-ups 2x12 · Face pulls 2x12 · Core circuit 2x12s — Your first upper body session. Focus on form, not load. Upper body and core strength directly improves your running posture and arm drive efficiency.

nut.d: 500ml water. Rest day — recovery is training.

sessions.det: 7km easy · 5:50–6:15/km · Conversational — Easy effort means conversational pace. This is recovery — your body adapts to training during rest, not during hard sessions.

nut.d: 500ml water. Rest day — recovery is training.

sessions.det: 8km easy · 5:50–6:15/km · Conversational — Easy effort means conversational pace. This is recovery — your body adapts to training during rest, not during hard sessions.
13km easy · 6:15–6:45/km · Walk breaks permitted · Gel at 6km for runs over 18km — A shorter long run this week — still builds time on feet and aerobic base without adding to accumulated fatigue.

nut.d: Porridge with banana and honey. 600ml water. 90–120 minutes before. Gel every 45min for runs over 90 minutes.
Full recovery meal within 60 minutes. Protein (30g), carbohydrates, vegetables. Your most important weekly nutrition window.

weeks.title: Week 2
weeks.note: Second week. Long run reaches 14km. Keep pace deliberately slow — you should be able to hold a conversation throughout. Most first-time marathoners make the mistake of running their easy days too hard and their long runs at the same pace. Avoid this.

nut.d: 500ml water. Rest day — recovery is training.

sessions.det: 7km easy · 5:50–6:15/km · Conversational — Keep this genuinely easy. The bulk of your aerobic development happens at low intensity and these runs build the engine that powers your quality sessions.

Back squat 3x12 · Romanian deadlift 3x12 · Bulgarian split squat 3x12 · Calf raise 3x15 · Core circuit 3x12s — Lower body strength is the engine of your running economy. These compound lifts build the hip and quad strength that keeps your form together in the final miles.

nut.d: 500ml water. Rest day — recovery is training.

sessions.det: 7km easy · 5:50–6:15/km · Conversational — Keep this genuinely easy. The bulk of your aerobic development happens at low intensity and these runs build the engine that powers your quality sessions.

nut.d: 500ml water. Rest day — recovery is training.

sessions.det: 8km easy · 5:50–6:15/km · Conversational — Keep this genuinely easy. The bulk of your aerobic development happens at low intensity and these runs build the engine that powers your quality sessions.

14km easy · 6:15–6:45/km · Walk breaks permitted · Gel at 7km for runs over 18km — The long run is the cornerstone of endurance training. Run it easy enough to hold a conversation and you'll finish feeling like you could go further — that's the goal.

nut.d: Porridge with banana and honey. 600ml water. 90–120 minutes before. Gel every 45min for runs over 90 minutes.
Full recovery meal within 60 minutes. Protein (30g), carbohydrates, vegetables. Your most important weekly nutrition window.

weeks.title: Recovery week
weeks.note: Recovery week. Long run drops to 10km. This is planned — use the lighter week to let the first fortnight's training consolidate.

nut.d: 500ml water. Rest day — recovery is training.

sessions.det: 6km easy · 5:50–6:15/km · Conversational — Easy effort means conversational pace. This is recovery — your body adapts to training during rest, not during hard sessions.

Hip thrust 2x12 · Nordic curl 2x12 · Lateral band walk 2x12 · Calf raise 3x15 · Stretching circuit 10min — A gentle posterior chain session and mobility work. During taper this is about keeping the body feeling loose and activated, not adding load.

nut.d: 500ml water. Rest day — recovery is training.

Full recovery meal within 60 minutes. Protein (30g), carbohydrates, vegetables. Your most important weekly nutrition window.

weeks.title: Week 4
weeks.note: Back to building. 16km long run. Your legs should feel good after last week's recovery. Start thinking about your gel strategy — practise carrying one on Sunday.

nut.d: 500ml water. Rest day — recovery is training.

sessions.det: 8km easy · 5:50–6:15/km · Conversational — Keep this genuinely easy. The bulk of your aerobic development happens at low intensity and these runs build the engine that powers your quality sessions.

weeks.title: Week 5
weeks.note: 18km long run. This is the first truly long run of the plan. Expect to feel tired in the final few kilometres. That tiredness is the training signal — it means the session is doing its job.

nut.d: 500ml water. Rest day — recovery is training.

sessions.det: 8km easy · 5:50–6:15/km · Conversational — Keep this genuinely easy. The bulk of your aerobic development happens at low intensity and these runs build the engine that powers your quality sessions.

Pull-ups 3x12 · DB row 3x12 · Overhead press 3x12 · Face pulls 3x12 · Core circuit 3x12s — Strong upper body and core keeps your posture tall when you tire. These sessions directly improve arm drive efficiency and reduce the slumping that costs you energy late in a race.

weeks.title: Week 7
weeks.note: 21km — half marathon distance. A significant milestone. You are running further than most recreational runners ever will. Keep the pace easy and enjoy covering the ground.

nut.d: 500ml water. Rest day — recovery is training.

weeks.note: 22km. Consistency is the key at this stage. Do not heroically extend the long run — run exactly what is prescribed.

weeks.title: Recovery week
weeks.note: Third recovery week. Back to 10km. You are halfway through the plan. The back half is where the real marathon-specific conditioning begins.

nut.d: 500ml water. Rest day — recovery is training.

sessions.det: 8km easy · 5:50–6:15/km · Conversational — Easy effort means conversational pace. This is recovery — your body adapts to training during rest, not during hard sessions.

nut.d: 500ml water. Rest day — recovery is training.

sessions.det: 2km WU · 5km steady (5:50–6:10/km) · 1km CD — Threshold pace is 'comfortably hard' — you could speak in short phrases but not hold a conversation. It builds your ability to sustain faster paces for longer.

weeks.note: 24km. The long runs are now long enough to require gel practice. Take one gel at 13km and practise the timing. Same gel brand you will use on race day.

weeks.note: 26km — the longest run in the first half of the back block. Take two gels: one at 13km, one at 20km. Note how you feel at each gel and in the following kilometre.

weeks.title: Week 13
weeks.note: 28km. The longest training run of most first-time marathon plans. You will feel the marathon becoming real this week. Three gels: 11km, 19km, 25km.

nut.d: 500ml water. Rest day — recovery is training.

sessions.det: 10km easy · 5:50–6:15/km · Conversational — Keep this genuinely easy. The bulk of your aerobic development happens at low intensity and these runs build the engine that powers your quality sessions.

weeks.title: Week 14
weeks.note: 30km. For most runners, this is the longest you will run before race day. The final 5–6km will be hard. This is normal and useful — you are learning how the marathon finishes.

nut.d: 500ml water. Rest day — recovery is training.

sessions.det: 11km easy · 5:50–6:15/km · Conversational — Keep this genuinely easy. The bulk of your aerobic development happens at low intensity and these runs build the engine that powers your quality sessions.

Pull-ups 4x5 · DB row 4x5 · Overhead press 4x5 · Face pulls 4x5 · Core circuit 4x5s — Upper body work while your legs taper — good timing. Pull movements build the postural strength that holds your form together at race pace.

nut.d: 500ml water. Rest day — recovery is training.

sessions.det: 2km WU · 8km steady (5:50–6:10/km) · 1km CD — Threshold work at peak phase. Your lactate threshold determines the pace you can sustain for an hour. These sessions push that ceiling up.

nut.d: 500ml water. Rest day — recovery is training.

weeks.title: Recovery week
weeks.note: Recovery week. 22km. Two more heavy weeks then the taper begins.

weeks.title: Week 16
weeks.note: 32km — peak long run. You will not run this far again before the marathon. Take gels at 11km, 19km, 25km and 29km if you have trained with four. Walk the final 3–4km of the run if needed.

nut.d: 500ml water. Rest day — recovery is training.

weeks.title: Taper week
weeks.note: Volume starts reducing. 26km long run. Your fitness is now set — the taper begins.

nut.d: 500ml water. Rest day — recovery is training.

sessions.det: 9km easy · 5:50–6:15/km · Conversational — Keep this genuinely easy. The bulk of your aerobic development happens at low intensity and these runs build the engine that powers your quality sessions.

nut.d: 500ml water. Rest day — recovery is training.

sessions.det: 2km WU · 6km steady (5:50–6:10/km) · 1km CD — A short threshold session to maintain the feel of race effort. Comfortably hard — not racing.

nut.d: 500ml water. Rest day — recovery is training.

sessions.det: 8km easy · 5:50–6:15/km · Conversational — Keep this genuinely easy. The bulk of your aerobic development happens at low intensity and these runs build the engine that powers your quality sessions.

26km easy · 6:15–6:45/km · Walk breaks permitted · Gel at 13km for runs over 18km — A confidence long run in taper — resist the urge to push. Arrive at the start line rested, not tired from a final fitness test.

nut.d: Porridge with banana and honey. 600ml water. 90–120 minutes before. Gel every 45min for runs over 90 minutes.
Full recovery meal within 60 minutes. Protein (30g), carbohydrates, vegetables. Your most important weekly nutrition window.

weeks.title: Taper week
weeks.note: Taper week 1. 19km long run. Your legs may feel oddly sluggish — this is normal taper adaptation. Trust it.

nut.d: 500ml water. Rest day — recovery is training.

weeks.title: Taper week
weeks.note: Taper week 2. 13km long run. By Thursday you should feel surprisingly good. Do not run more than prescribed.

nut.d: 500ml water. Rest day — recovery is training.

sessions.det: 6km easy · 5:50–6:15/km · Conversational — Easy effort means conversational pace. This is recovery — your body adapts to training during rest, not during hard sessions.

Hip thrust 2x12 · Nordic curl 2x12 · Lateral band walk 2x12 · Calf raise 3x15 · Stretching circuit 10min — A gentle posterior chain session and mobility work. During taper this is about keeping the body feeling loose and activated, not adding load.

nut.d: 500ml water. Rest day — recovery is training.

sessions.det: 6km easy · 5:50–6:15/km · Conversational — Easy effort means conversational pace. This is recovery — your body adapts to training during rest, not during hard sessions.

nut.d: 500ml water. Rest day — recovery is training.

sessions.det: 8km easy · 5:50–6:15/km · Conversational — Easy effort means conversational pace. This is recovery — your body adapts to training during rest, not during hard sessions.
13km easy · 6:15–6:45/km · Walk breaks permitted · Gel at 6km for runs over 18km — A shorter long run this week — still builds time on feet and aerobic base without adding to accumulated fatigue.

nut.d: Porridge with banana and honey. 600ml water. 90–120 minutes before. Gel every 45min for runs over 90 minutes.
Full recovery meal within 60 minutes. Protein (30g), carbohydrates, vegetables. Your most important weekly nutrition window.

weeks.title: Week 20
weeks.note: Race week. 42.2km. You are ready. Run the first half at the pace that feels easy. Walk through every water station. Trust your long runs.

nut.d: 500ml water. Rest day — recovery is training.

sessions.det: 5km easy · 5:50–6:15/km · Conversational — Keep this genuinely easy. The bulk of your aerobic development happens at low intensity and these runs build the engine that powers your quality sessions.

nut.d: 500ml water. Rest day — recovery is training.

sessions.det: 5km easy · 5:50–6:15/km · Conversational — Keep this genuinely easy. The bulk of your aerobic development happens at low intensity and these runs build the engine that powers your quality sessions.

nut.d: 500ml water. Rest day — recovery is training.

sessions.det: 3km easy · 5:50–6:15/km · Conversational — Keep this genuinely easy. The bulk of your aerobic development happens at low intensity and these runs build the engine that powers your quality sessions.

Marathon race · Start at 6:20/km · Walk every water station · Gel at 10km/21km/29km/35km · Trust your training — Race day. Trust your training. Start conservatively — most races are won or lost in the second half.

nut.d: Large bowl of porridge with banana and honey, or bagel with peanut butter. 600ml water. This is the most important pre-race meal of your life — eat something you have eaten before every long run.
400ml water or isotonic drink. Sip steadily. If you use caffeine, consider 200mg now.
Optional: small banana or gel if feeling low on energy. 200ml water.
10–15min easy walk or jog. Strides only if experienced at this. Get to the start corral early.
Gel with a full cup of water. Do not take gels without water. Use the same gels you trained with — no experiments.
Gel with water at halfway. You should still feel good here — if you feel bad at halfway, you went out too fast.
Gel with water. This is when the marathon gets hard. Stay disciplined with your pace — do not speed up yet.
Fourth gel if you have trained with four and are fading. Water at every station from 35km to the finish.
Isotonic drink or water at the finish line. 500ml in the first 10 minutes. Keep moving — walk for at least 15 minutes.
Protein bar and banana at minimum. 30g protein immediately. Your muscles are damaged — start repair now.

Within 2 hours: 40g protein, high carbohydrates, sodium to replace sweat losses. Drink 500ml of water or isotonic drink per hour for the next 4 hours. You have just run 42.2km — the recovery starts now.
